channel.title: Tourism on Bali Kini
channel.description: Recent content in Tourism on Bali Kini

item.title: Bali Tourism June 2026: 553K Visitors in April, Target 6.6M for the Year
item.description:
Bali welcomed 553,000 foreign visitors in April 2026, according to the latest data from BPS Bali (Statistics Indonesia). While this represents a 6.41% decline year-on-year, the island’s tourism economy tells a more nuanced story.

The Visitor Paradox

Despite fewer international arrivals, hotel occupancy rates across Bali actually increased during the same period. Analysts attribute this anomaly to a surge in domestic tourism — Indonesian travelers from Java and other islands filling the rooms that fewer foreign visitors left empty.

item.title: 7 Best Sunset Spots in Bali You Must Visit in 2026
item.description:
Bali’s late afternoons possess a particular kind of magic. As the sun tilts westward, the sky transforms into a vast canvas of orange, pink and violet — a spectacle photographed a million times, yet never losing its power. If you’re in Bali and wondering where to spend your evening, here are the seven sunset spots that consistently deliver.

1. Tanah Lot — The Temple on the Sea

Nothing rivals Tanah Lot at golden hour. The ancient sea temple, perched on an isolated rock formation separated from the mainland at high tide, becomes a perfect silhouette as the sun drops behind it. The combination of crashing Indian Ocean waves, flickering temple offerings, and a sky ablaze with colour makes this one of the most photographed scenes in all of Asia.

item.title: Galungan 2026: The Complete Guide for Visitors and Locals
item.description:
Galungan 2026 falls on June 17, 2026, followed by Kuningan on June 27, 2026. For the Balinese, this is the most sacred moment in the Hindu Balinese calendar — and for visitors, it is simply the best time to see Bali in its most authentic form.

What Is Galungan?

Galungan celebrates the victory of dharma (good) over adharma (evil). The festival occurs every 210 days according to the Balinese Pawukon calendar. For 10 days — from Galungan to Kuningan — the entire island is adorned with penjor: tall curved bamboo poles decorated with coconut leaves, fruits, and yellow cloth lining every road and temple entrance.
